sections.title: Advanced Chemistry Workflows on Magne
sections.content:
Advanced Chemistry Workflows on Magne

Welcome to qBraid's course on the Microsoft Chemistry QDK. This course will equip you with the necessary skills and tools to use the Microsoft Chemistry QDK confidently. You will learn the complete workflow for quantum chemistry simulations: starting with classical preprocessing techniques to reduce problem size (active space selection, Hamiltonian construction), then encoding molecular systems as qubits using efficient mappings, preparing quantum states, and implementing Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) algorithms to calculate ground-state energies. The course emphasizes hands-on experience with QDK tooling through practical notebooks, culminating in a complete demonstration where you implement QPE on a model Hamiltonian. Rather than focusing on theoretical foundations, the curriculum prioritizes operational knowledge; you will learn how to actually use these tools for real computational chemistry workflows, with all techniques demonstrated using Microsoft's quantum software stack.

Chapter 0: Getting Started

This chapter gets your environment ready and gives you the conceptual foundation for the rest of the course. By the end, you will have the Chemistry QDK running locally and a clear picture of how the full simulation workflow fits together. The final cell in this chapter must pass before proceeding to Chapter 1.

What you'll learn

- How to set up your local environment: clone the course repo and install the Chemistry QDK (prereq gate) - Where the Chemistry QDK sits within the Microsoft quantum software stack - How the Chemistry QDK differs from the base QDK: domain-specific typed objects and a chemistry-first workflow - The algorithm registry: one interface to create, configure, and discover every component - What a complete end-to-end molecular simulation looks like, from structure input to energy output

The Microsoft quantum software stack

The Chemistry QDK is one layer in a broader Microsoft quantum software stack. Understanding where it sits helps you know which tool to reach for at each stage of a workflow.
sections.editNote: Merged with following section 'The Microsoft quantum software stack'

sections.title: Using GitHub Copilot with the Chemistry QDK
sections.content:
Using GitHub Copilot with the Chemistry QDK

GitHub Copilot is a VS Code extension that provides AI-assisted code completion, and it works particularly well with the Chemistry QDK's registry-based API. Because the entire QDK follows a single consistent pattern — `available()`, `create()`, `inspect_settings()` — Copilot can learn the idiom quickly and complete method names, setting keys, and workflow sequences with high accuracy. You do not need to memorize every algorithm name or parameter: start typing `create("orbital_localizer",` and Copilot will suggest valid implementation names and keyword arguments based on your surrounding context. The most effective way to use Copilot here is through inline comments that describe your intent. For example, writing `# apply MP2 natural orbital localization to the valence wavefunction` before a blank line will usually produce a complete, correct `create(...)` + `.run(...)` call. Similarly, comments like `# compare orbital summaries before and after localization` or `# run the stability checker and print whether the solution is internal and external stable` produce idiomatic QDK code. Copilot is less reliable for the non-obvious parts — like the VVHV localizer requiring all virtual indices rather than just the active space indices — so use `inspect_settings()` and the course exercises to verify its suggestions at decision points. To enable Copilot in VS Code, install the **GitHub Copilot** extension from the VS Code marketplace and sign in with a GitHub account that has Copilot access. Once active, completions appear inline as you type — press `Tab` to accept. You can also open the Copilot Chat panel (`Ctrl+Shift+I` / `Cmd+Shift+I`) to ask questions about the QDK directly. Useful prompts include: *"What settings does the qdk_autocas active space selector expose?"*, *"Show me the full pattern to construct a Hamiltonian from a localized wavefunction"*, or *"What does get_summary() return for a Wavefunction object?"*. Copilot Chat can also explain unfamiliar QDK output and suggest next steps in a workflow, making it a useful companion for the more open-ended exercises in later chapters.

sections.title: End-to-end workflow preview
sections.content:
End-to-end workflow preview

Before diving into each component, here is the complete molecular simulation workflow you will build over this course. Each chapter covers one stage. The full workflow in code looks like this — you will build familiarity with each line over the next six chapters. Run the next cell to get a sneak peek of what we will be diving deep into each chapter, learning about various knobs to turn, and understanding time / memory / qubit / accuracy tradeoffs. In fact, we will examine the minimal choices we've made in the example below for speed in detail in the coming chapters.

sections.title: The registry: one interface for everything
sections.content:
The registry: one interface for everything

Every algorithm in `qdk-chemistry` — SCF solvers, active space selectors, qubit mappers, QPE — is created through a single registry. There are no scattered constructors or module-specific imports to remember. The pattern is always: ``` from qdk_chemistry.algorithms import available, create, inspect_settings create(algorithm_type, algorithm_name, **settings) ``` This design means you can swap implementations (e.g. different active space selectors) by changing one string, and discover what's available without reading source code.

sections.title: Part 1: What's in the registry?
sections.content:
Part 1: What's in the registry?

`available()` with no arguments returns a dict of every registered algorithm type and its implementations. **Task**: Run the cell. Note which algorithm types appear. How many types are there? How many implementations does each have?

sections.title: Part 2: Plugins enable runtime execution
sections.content:
Part 2: Plugins enable runtime execution

Importing the plugin modules doesn't add new names to the registry — the registry already knows about all algorithm types and implementations. What plugins provide is the **runtime backend**: without `plugins.pyscf`, calling `scf_solver.run()` would fail even though `scf_solver` appears in `available()`. Without `plugins.qiskit`, state preparation and qubit mapping would be unavailable at runtime. This is an intentional design: dependencies (PySCF, Qiskit) are optional. The registry API is stable regardless of which backends are installed. **Task**: Import both plugins. Confirm the registry is unchanged, then verify what the plugins actually enable by checking which implementations require them to run.

sections.title: Part 3: Inspecting a single algorithm type
sections.content:
Part 3: Inspecting a single algorithm type

`available(algorithm_type)` narrows the query to one type. This is useful when you know what you want and just need to see the options. **Task**: Use `available()` to list implementations for `scf_solver` and `active_space_selector`.

sections.title: Part 4: Reading algorithm settings
sections.content:
Part 4: Reading algorithm settings

Every algorithm exposes its configurable parameters through a settings interface. `inspect_settings(type, name)` returns a structured list of `(name, type, default, description, limits)` tuples. `print_settings(type, name)` prints a formatted table. **Task**: Inspect the settings for both SCF solver implementations. Identify: - Which parameter controls the SCF method (HF vs DFT)? - What is the default convergence threshold? - How do the settings differ between the qdk and pyscf implementations?

notebookCells.heading: Print settings table for SCF solver

sections.title: Part 5: Creating an algorithm instance
sections.content:
Part 5: Creating an algorithm instance

`create(type, name)` returns an algorithm instance. Settings can be passed at creation time as keyword arguments, or updated afterwards via `instance.settings().update(...)`. **Task**: Create two SCF solvers — one with default settings, one with `max_iterations=100` and `convergence_threshold=1e-8`. Confirm the settings took effect by reading them back.

notebookCells.heading: Create algorithm instances with custom settings

sections.title: Prerequisite: environment check
sections.content:
Prerequisite: environment check

Run the cell below. It checks that: 1. qdk-chemistry imports correctly 2. Both plugins load without error 3. The registry contains the expected algorithm types 4. The N₂ data file used throughout this course is accessible All assertions must pass before proceeding to Chapter 1.

sections.title: Chapter 1: Molecular Input & Data Structures
sections.content:
Chapter 1: Molecular Input & Data Structures

What you'll learn

- How to load molecule data from file formats and build Structure objects within the QDK - How to run an SCF and DFT calculation end-to-end - The QDK's shared vocabulary of typed objects — Structure, Wavefunction, Orbitals — and how they flow between pipeline steps - What each algorithm consumes and returns — and how to avoid common type confusion errors - How the Chemistry QDK connects with Qiskit, PennyLane, OpenFermion, and RDKit

The molecule for this course

We use **stretched N₂** throughout — nitrogen with a bond length of 1.27 Å (vs. equilibrium ~1.10 Å). The stretched geometry introduces strong multi-reference character, meaning no single electronic configuration adequately describes the ground state. This makes it an ideal system for exercising the full QDK workflow.

sections.title: Part 1: Loading a molecular structure
sections.content:
Part 1: Loading a molecular structure

`Structure` is the entry point for all chemistry in the QDK. It wraps atomic coordinates and exposes methods for visualization and conversion. The most common input route is an XYZ file via `Structure.from_xyz_file()`. You can also build a `Structure` directly from coordinates in memory using `Structure(coordinates=coords, symbols=symbols)` — useful when working programmatically or integrating with RDKit (see the interoperability section at the end of this chapter). **Note**: the constructor takes coordinates in **Bohr**. XYZ files are in Angstrom and are converted automatically by `from_xyz_file()`. In the code snippet Load stretched N₂ from the XYZ file. Then build the same molecule directly from coordinates (in Bohr) and confirm they match.

notebookCells.heading: Load N₂ structure from file and from coordinates

sections.title: Part 2: Running SCF with a minimal basis
sections.content:
Part 2: Running SCF with a minimal basis

SCF (self-consistent field) is the classical starting point for any quantum chemistry workflow. It produces: - A Hartree-Fock energy (the best single-determinant approximation to the ground state) - A Wavefunction object containing the molecular orbitals The `scf_solver` is created via the registry. Its `run()` method takes the structure plus calculation parameters. We will run SCF on stretched N₂ with the `sto-3g` basis.

sections.title: Part 3: Exploring the Wavefunction and Orbitals objects
sections.content:
Part 3: Exploring the Wavefunction and Orbitals objects

The `Wavefunction` returned by SCF is the primary data object that flows through the rest of the pipeline. It contains the molecular orbitals, electron counts, and (later) determinant expansions. Let's explore what the STO-3G wavefunction contains. Note the number of occupied and virtual orbitals.

notebookCells.heading: Explore the Wavefunction and Orbitals objects

sections.title: Part 4: Comparing basis sets
sections.content:
Part 4: Comparing basis sets

Basis set choice determines how many orbitals are in the problem — and directly controls the cost of every subsequent step (active space selection, Hamiltonian construction, qubit count). The `cc-pVDZ` basis is a standard double-zeta basis that is significantly larger than STO-3G and more chemically accurate. It is the default choice for the rest of this course. Now let's run SCF again with `cc-pvdz`. Compare the HF energies and orbital counts from both runs.

notebookCells.heading: Run SCF with cc-pVDZ and compare energies

quiz.note: Question hosted in qBook question bank (ID: ch1-basisset-rajq65)
quiz.editNote: extracted from TODO stub, not from question bank

sections.title: Part 5: SCF settings and DFT
sections.content:
Part 5: SCF settings and DFT

The SCF solver's `method` setting switches between Hartree-Fock (`"hf"`) and DFT (`"dft"`). Settings are passed as keyword arguments to `create()`. Let's run a DFT calculation on N₂ with `cc-pvdz`. Fill in the `???` below, then compare the DFT and HF energies. Which is lower, and why? You can even explore how the DFT energy changes as you explore different functionals at different steps on Jacobb's ladder (see image below). Typically, as you go up the ladder, your energy will get lower, capturing more of the correlation energy. (Image taken from Microsoft Blogs).

notebookCells.heading: Run DFT and compare with Hartree-Fock

quiz.note: Question hosted in qBook question bank (ID: ch1-dft-code-c2g0dy)
quiz.editNote: extracted from TODO stub, not from question bank

sections.title: Typed objects and data flow
sections.content:
Typed objects and data flow

Every step in the Chemistry QDK pipeline produces and consumes strongly-typed objects. Understanding this map prevents the most common errors — passing an `Orbitals` object where a `Wavefunction` is expected, or forgetting to call `.get_orbitals()` before passing to a Hamiltonian constructor. The diagram below walks you through the most important component of the overall Microsoft Chemistry QDK. **Key rules:** - orbital_localizer and active_space_selector both take and return Wavefunction — not Orbitals - hamiltonian_constructor takes Orbitals — call .get_orbitals() first - state_prep takes Wavefunction (the CASCI/ASCI result), not Orbitals - Core energy (hamiltonian.get_core_energy()) must be added to QPE raw energy to get the total

sections.title: Interoperability
sections.content:
Interoperability

The Chemistry QDK is designed to work alongside other quantum software stacks. The typed objects produced at each pipeline stage can be exported to — or built from — external libraries. | External stack | Integration point | How | |---|---|---| | **Qiskit** | `QubitHamiltonian`, `Circuit`, circuit executor | Native: `qubit_mapper` produces a Qiskit `SparsePauliOp`; `qiskit_aer_simulator` runs circuits | | **Cirq** | `Circuit` | Via QASM3 export: `circuit.get_qasm()` → `cirq.from_qasm()` | | **PennyLane** | `Circuit` | Via QASM3 export: `circuit.get_qasm()` → PennyLane `from_qasm()` | | **OpenFermion** | `Hamiltonian` | Export one- and two-body integrals via `get_one_body_integrals()` / `get_two_body_integrals()` | | **RDKit** | `Structure` input | Build `Structure` from SMILES via `Structure(coordinates=coords, symbols=symbols)` | The cell below demonstrates the Qiskit integration — the qubit mapper produces a native Qiskit `SparsePauliOp` that can be passed directly into Qiskit's `Estimator`, VQE, or any other Qiskit primitive. Now, let's test that you fully understand this interoperability feature and its strengths. In addition to easily moving between QDK and other quantum software types, you could also bring in your fermionic or qubit Hamiltonian from other frameworks, and use the Microsoft QDK to calculate energies and properties. In the code snippet below, the Qiskit code defines the Qubit Hamiltonian for the hydrogen molecule, in the ground state, with a STO-3G basis. It also has been mapped with parity in mind, so it only needs 2 qubits. We see in this example how you can calculate the energy of the molecule using iterative QPE, which is unique to the Microsoft QDK. Right now, the QPE code is provided for you, but we will learn about each component in this course. Fill in the ```???```, and then answer the question below on qBook with the final energy you get.

notebookCells.heading: Import Qiskit SparsePauliOp into the QDK

quiz.note: Question hosted in qBook question bank (ID: ch1-interop-wkqhy9)
quiz.editNote: extracted from TODO stub, not from question bank

sections.title: Summary
sections.content:
Summary

**Key patterns:** ``` # Load structure structure = Structure.from_xyz_file(path) structure = Structure(coordinates=coords_in_bohr, symbols=["N", "N"]) # SCF energy, wfn = create("scf_solver").run(structure, charge=0, spin_multiplicity=1, basis_or_guess="cc-pvdz") # Navigate types correctly orbitals = wfn.get_orbitals() # Wavefunction → Orbitals hamiltonian = create("hamiltonian_constructor").run(orbitals) # Orbitals → Hamiltonian total_energy = result.raw_energy + hamiltonian.get_core_energy() # always add core energy ``` In this chapter you: - Mapped the full typed object pipeline: Structure → Wavefunction → Orbitals → Hamiltonian → QubitHamiltonian → Circuit → QpeResult - Loaded Structure from XYZ file and from coordinates directly — noting that the constructor takes Bohr, while XYZ files use Angstrom - Ran SCF with sto-3g and cc-pvdz and compared energies and orbital counts - Ran DFT and compared against HF - Saw how the QDK connects to RDKit, OpenFermion, Qiskit, and PennyLane The `cc-pvdz` wavefunction (`wfn_dz`) is the starting point for Chapter 2. You also tested your understanding of various tools and features by answering questions on qBook.

chapters.title: Chapter 2: Classical Functionality: SCF, Localization, Stability

sections.title: Chapter 2: Classical Functionality: SCF, Localization, Stability
sections.content:
Chapter 2: Classical Functionality: SCF, Localization, Stability

What you'll learn

- Why canonical SCF orbitals are often unsuitable for quantum workflows — and how localization fixes this - The built-in QDK localizers (`qdk_mp2_natural_orbitals`, `qdk_pipek_mezey`, `qdk_vvhv`) and the PySCF plugin localizer (Foster-Boys, Edmiston-Ruedenberg) - How to interpret `get_summary()` to compare orbital character before and after localization - What the stability checker does, when to use it, and how stability instability differs from convergence failure

Why localize?

Canonical molecular orbitals from SCF are delocalized across the entire molecule — they're eigenstates of the Fock operator, optimized for energy, not for physical interpretability. For active space selection, delocalized orbitals obscure which electrons are strongly correlated. Localization rotates the orbital basis to produce spatially compact, chemically meaningful orbitals without changing the total energy or wavefunction.

sections.title: Part 1: Reducing to the valence space
sections.content:
Part 1: Reducing to the valence space

Before localizing, we reduce to the **valence space** — the chemically relevant frontier orbitals. The N₂ cc-pvdz calculation produces 28 orbitals, most of which are high-energy virtuals that contribute negligibly to chemistry. Working in the valence space is a prerequisite for localization in this workflow. `compute_valence_space_parameters()` is a utility that computes the number of valence electrons and orbitals for a given wavefunction. The `qdk_valence` active space selector then applies these to extract the valence subspace. Here, let's compute the valence space parameters for stretched N₂ and apply the valence selector. Take a look at the result.

sections.title: Part 2: What localizers are available?
sections.content:
Part 2: What localizers are available?

Now, let's use `available()` to list all registered orbital localizers. Take a minute to inspect the settings for each one.

sections.title: Part 3: Applying MP2 natural orbital localization
sections.content:
Part 3: Applying MP2 natural orbital localization

`qdk_mp2_natural_orbitals` uses second-order Møller-Plesset perturbation theory to compute natural orbitals — eigenstates of the correlated one-particle density matrix. These have fractional occupations that directly indicate correlation strength: occupations far from 0 or 2 flag strongly correlated orbitals. The localizer's `run()` method takes the wavefunction and the active space index range (occupied start, virtual end). We will apply MP2 natural orbital localization to the valence space, and compare the orbital summary before and after.

sections.title: Part 4: Comparing localization methods
sections.content:
Part 4: Comparing localization methods

The QDK provides two additional localizers with different objectives: - **`qdk_pipek_mezey`**: Maximizes orbital localization by minimizing the spread of each orbital over atomic centers. Produces orbitals resembling lone pairs and bonds. Takes the active space indices. - **`qdk_vvhv`**: Valence-virtual hybrid localizer. Unlike the others, it requires **all virtual orbital indices** (from `n_alpha_electrons` to `num_molecular_orbitals-1`) — not just the active space. It localizes the entire virtual manifold, which is useful when downstream steps need well-localized virtual orbitals beyond the active space. We will apply both localizers to `wfn_valence`, and compare all three orbital summaries side by side.

sections.title: Part 5: PySCF plugin localizer
sections.content:
Part 5: PySCF plugin localizer

The three built-in QDK localizers (`qdk_mp2_natural_orbitals`, `qdk_pipek_mezey`, `qdk_vvhv`) cover the most common workflows. Importing the PySCF plugin registers an additional `"pyscf_multi"` localizer that exposes PySCF's full localization suite via a `method` setting: | `method` | Algorithm | |---|---| | `"pipek-mezey"` | Pipek-Mezey (default) | | `"foster-boys"` | Foster-Boys | | `"edmiston-ruedenberg"` | Edmiston-Ruedenberg | | `"cholesky"` | Cholesky-based | In the code cell below, we will apply Foster-Boys localization via the PySCF plugin and compare the orbital summary with the built-in Pipek-Mezey result from Part 4. To run the code, fill in the ```???``` first, and then answer the question below.

notebookCells.heading: Apply Foster-Boys localization via PySCF plugin

quiz.note: Question hosted in qBook question bank (ID: ch2-foster-boys-tw78sa)
quiz.editNote: extracted from TODO stub, not from question bank

sections.title: Part 6: SCF stability check
sections.content:
Part 6: SCF stability check

For some molecules, the Hartree-Fock wavefunction is not the true variational minimum — the SCF procedure can converge to a saddle point rather than a ground state. The stability checker tests whether the converged solution is stable against orbital rotations. This is especially relevant for stretched bonds and open-shell systems, where restricted HF often yields an unstable solution. For stretched N₂, this check is important: broken-symmetry solutions may exist. > **Stability instability vs convergence failure**: these are distinct problems. > - *Convergence failure*: SCF never reaches a self-consistent solution — the iterations don't settle. Fix by tightening thresholds, changing the initial guess, or switching the SCF algorithm. > - *Stability instability*: SCF converged, but to a saddle point — a lower-energy solution exists via an orbital rotation. The stability checker diagnoses this *after* a successful convergence. > Stretched N₂ is a canonical example of the second case: SCF converges cleanly, but the restricted solution is not the ground state. **Task**: Run the stability checker on the HF wavefunction. Is it stable? What does the checker return?

quiz.note: Question hosted in qBook question bank (ID: ch2-local-frq-26yvxg)
quiz.editNote: extracted from TODO stub, not from question bank

sections.title: Summary
sections.content:
Summary

In this chapter you: - Used `compute_valence_space_parameters()` and `qdk_valence` to reduce to the chemically relevant orbital subspace - Applied three orbital localizers and compared their output via `get_summary()` - Explored the PySCF plugin localizer (Foster-Boys, Edmiston-Ruedenberg) and ran the stability checker to test whether the HF wavefunction is a true energy minimum The MP2-localized valence wavefunction (`wfn_mp2`) is the recommended input for active space selection in Chapter 3. **Key pattern to remember:** ```python num_val_e, num_val_o = compute_valence_space_parameters(wfn_hf, charge=0) wfn_valence = create("active_space_selector", "qdk_valence", num_active_electrons=num_val_e, num_active_orbitals=num_val_o).run(wfn_hf) indices = wfn_valence.get_orbitals().get_active_space_indices() wfn_loc = create("orbital_localizer", "qdk_mp2_natural_orbitals").run(wfn_valence, *indices) ```

sections.title: Chapter 3: Active Space Selection
sections.content:
Chapter 3: Active Space Selection

What you'll learn

- Why the choice of active space directly determines qubit cost — and why there's no single right answer - The full menu of QDK active space selectors: `qdk_valence`, `pyscf_avas`, `qdk_occupation`, `qdk_autocas`, and `qdk_autocas_eos` - When each method is appropriate and how to interpret what they return - How to compare active spaces across methods by their qubit footprint

The fundamental tradeoff

Active space selection determines how expensive your quantum calculation will be. Every spatial orbital you include costs **two qubits** (one per spin). A 10-orbital active space needs 20 qubits; a 14-orbital space needs 28. The exponential scaling of exact diagonalization means this difference is not cosmetic. The goal is to find the **smallest active space that still captures the strongly correlated physics**. Different selectors make different assumptions about what "strongly correlated" means: | Selector | Basis for selection | Requires | |---|---|---| | `qdk_valence` | Valence shell heuristic | HF wavefunction | | `pyscf_avas` | AO-to-MO projection overlap | HF wavefunction | | `qdk_occupation` | Natural orbital occupation numbers | SCI wavefunction with 1-RDM | | `qdk_autocas` | Single-orbital entanglement entropy | SCI wavefunction with 1- and 2-RDM | | `qdk_autocas_eos` | Entropy of substitution (EOS) | SCI wavefunction with 1- and 2-RDM |

notebookCells.heading: Setup: SCF, valence space, and MP2 localization
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Setup: SCF, valence space, and MP2 localization

sections.title: Part 1: What selectors are available?
sections.content:
Part 1: What selectors are available?

`available("active_space_selector")` lists every registered selector. `print_settings()` shows each one's configurable parameters. Run the cell to see them all.

notebookCells.heading: List available active space selectors
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: List available active space selectors

sections.title: Part 2: qdk_valence -- manual tuning
sections.content:
Part 2: qdk_valence — manual tuning

`qdk_valence` is the simplest selector: it takes explicit electron and orbital counts and carves out that subspace from the SCF wavefunction. `compute_valence_space_parameters()` infers sensible defaults, but those are just a starting point. For stretched N₂ with cc-pvdz, the valence heuristic returns 10 electrons in 8 orbitals. You can shrink to (6e, 6o) to focus on the σ-system only, or expand to (10e, 10o) to include more virtuals. Each choice has a different qubit cost. Let's run three `qdk_valence` configurations and compare their qubit footprints.

notebookCells.heading: Three manual active space choices for stretched N₂
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Three manual active space choices for stretched N₂

sections.title: Part 3: pyscf_avas -- chemistry-guided selection
sections.content:
Part 3: pyscf_avas — chemistry-guided selection

`pyscf_avas` (Atomic Valence Active Space) takes a different approach to selection: instead of heuristics or orbital entropy, it asks *which atomic orbital character do you want in the active space?* You provide a list of AO labels (e.g., `["N 2s", "N 2p"]`), and AVAS projects those onto the molecular orbital basis, retaining the MOs with the largest overlap weight. This is the most **chemistry-guided** option: you are directly specifying the bonding/antibonding character you want the active space to capture. For N₂ dissociation, including both 2s and 2p picks up the σ and π bonds. Like `qdk_valence`, AVAS works directly from the HF wavefunction — no SCI step needed. Fill in the missing AO label in the cell below.

notebookCells.heading: AVAS: atomic-orbital-guided selection
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: AVAS: atomic-orbital-guided selection

sections.title: Part 4: Entropy-based selection -- qdk_autocas and qdk_autocas_eos
sections.content:
Part 4: Entropy-based selection — qdk_autocas and qdk_autocas_eos

The autoCAS selectors are the most physically motivated option. They compute **single-orbital entanglement entropies** from a multi-configuration wavefunction: orbitals with high entropy are strongly entangled with the rest of the system and belong in the active space; orbitals near zero can be frozen. This requires two prerequisites: 1. A classical Hamiltonian for the active space (via `hamiltonian_constructor`) 2. A SCI wavefunction with 1-RDM and 2-RDM (via `multi_configuration_calculator`, here MACIS ASCI) `qdk_autocas_eos` adds an *entropy of substitution* step: it tests whether swapping each active orbital changes the entropy landscape, catching cases the standard criterion misses. In the cells below, we build the Hamiltonian from `wfn_mp2`, run MACIS ASCI with RDMs, then apply both autoCAS methods. Compare what each selects.

notebookCells.heading: Build Hamiltonian and run MACIS ASCI with RDMs
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Build Hamiltonian and run MACIS ASCI with RDMs

notebookCells.heading: Apply standard autoCAS: entropy-based selection
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Apply standard autoCAS: entropy-based selection

notebookCells.heading: Apply autoCAS-EOS: entropy-of-substitution refinement
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Apply autoCAS-EOS: entropy-of-substitution refinement

quiz.note: Question hosted in qBook question bank (ID: ch3-q1)

sections.title: Part 5: qdk_occupation -- occupation-based selection
sections.content:
Part 5: qdk_occupation — occupation-based selection

`qdk_occupation` selects orbitals based on their **natural orbital occupation numbers** from the 1-RDM. In a strongly correlated system, orbitals with occupations near 1 (far from the fully-occupied value of 2 or fully-empty value of 0) are partially filled — a direct indicator of correlation. This is complementary to entropy: entropy measures *entanglement* between an orbital and the rest; occupation numbers measure the orbital's *partial filling*. For stretched N₂, the σ-bonding and σ*-antibonding orbitals should both show occupation near 1. Like the autoCAS methods, it reads from the 1-RDM and so takes the SCI wavefunction from Part 4. Now, let's apply `qdk_occupation` to `wfn_sci`. Inspect the threshold setting, then compare to the autoCAS result.

notebookCells.heading: Apply occupation-based selection
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Apply occupation-based selection

sections.title: Part 6: Cost vs. accuracy comparison
sections.content:
Part 6: Cost vs. accuracy comparison

Each selector has made a different bet about which orbitals matter. Now compare them on two dimensions: qubit cost and energy accuracy. The first cell collects qubit counts for all seven selections. The second runs exact diagonalization on the four selections that share the same MP2-localized orbital basis — so their energies are directly comparable. This is the cost-vs-accuracy tradeoff the syllabus calls out: a smaller active space is cheaper but misses correlation energy.

notebookCells.heading: Qubit cost: all selectors compared
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Qubit cost: all selectors compared

notebookCells.heading: Energy vs. cost: exact diagonalization across active space sizes
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Energy vs. cost: exact diagonalization across active space sizes

sections.title: Summary
sections.content:
Summary

In this chapter you: - Surveyed all five active space selectors and their settings - Applied `qdk_valence` directly from the HF wavefunction with three manual configurations - Applied `pyscf_avas` with AO labels (`"N 2s"`, `"N 2p"`) to select by atomic character from the HF wavefunction - Built a MACIS ASCI SCI wavefunction with 1- and 2-RDM, then ran entropy-based (`qdk_autocas`, `qdk_autocas_eos`) and occupation-based (`qdk_occupation`) selectors - Compared all selections by qubit cost for stretched N₂ with cc-pvdz The autoCAS-EOS wavefunction (`wfn_eos`) is the recommended output to carry forward: it uses the most principled selection criterion and is the input to Hamiltonian construction in Chapter 4. **Key pattern:** ```python # Build Hamiltonian → run CASCI with RDMs → autoCAS-EOS hamiltonian_constructor = create("hamiltonian_constructor") loc_hamiltonian = hamiltonian_constructor.run(wfn_mp2.get_orbitals()) macis = create("multi_configuration_calculator", "macis_asci", calculate_one_rdm=True, calculate_two_rdm=True) macis.settings().set("core_selection_strategy", "fixed") _, wfn_sci = macis.run(loc_hamiltonian, *wfn_mp2.get_active_num_electrons()) wfn_active = create("active_space_selector", "qdk_autocas_eos").run(wfn_sci) ```

sections.title: Chapter 4: Hamiltonian Construction & Qubit Mapping
sections.content:
Chapter 4: Hamiltonian Construction & Qubit Mapping

What you'll learn

- What information the classical Hamiltonian encodes and how integral counts scale with active space size - How to build Hamiltonians for different active spaces and read `get_summary()` diagnostically - The three qubit encodings available in the QDK: Jordan-Wigner, Bravyi-Kitaev, and Parity - How to verify that all encodings are equivalent via exact diagonalization - How the Schatten norm connects your qubit Hamiltonian directly to QPE circuit cost - How to build model Hamiltonians (Ising, Heisenberg, Hubbard) using `LatticeGraph` for hardware benchmarking

From active space to qubits

Chapter 3 produced an active space (via autoCAS-EOS) and showed that choosing fewer orbitals saves qubits. This chapter takes the next step: converting that active space into a qubit Hamiltonian that a quantum computer can act on. The conversion has two stages. First, the classical Hamiltonian is constructed from the one- and two-body integrals of the active orbital basis. Second, a qubit mapper applies a fermion-to-qubit encoding that rewrites fermionic creation and annihilation operators as Pauli strings. Different encodings distribute fermionic anti-commutation relations differently across qubits — they are unitarily equivalent but can differ in circuit depth for specific hardware.

notebookCells.heading: Setup: SCF → valence → MP2 → autoCAS-EOS pipeline
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Setup: SCF → valence → MP2 → autoCAS-EOS pipeline

sections.title: Part 1: Building the active space Hamiltonian
sections.content:
Part 1: Building the active space Hamiltonian

The `hamiltonian_constructor` takes an `Orbitals` object (from any wavefunction) and computes the one- and two-body integrals in that orbital basis. The result is a classical Hamiltonian object that stores everything needed for both exact diagonalization and qubit mapping. `get_summary()` exposes four diagnostically useful fields: active orbital count (qubit cost), core energy (constant offset folded into the qubit identity term), and the one- and two-body integral counts with a sparsity indicator (how many are above a threshold).

notebookCells.heading: Inspect hamiltonian_constructor settings
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Inspect hamiltonian_constructor settings

notebookCells.heading: Build the active space Hamiltonian and interpret the summary
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Build the active space Hamiltonian and interpret the summary

sections.title: Part 2: Active space size and integral scaling
sections.content:
Part 2: Active space size and integral scaling

Two-body integrals $h_{pqrs}$ have four orbital indices and scale as n⁴ in the number of active orbitals. Going from 8 to 4 orbitals reduces the two-body table from 4096 to 256 entries — a 16× reduction. After qubit mapping, this translates directly into fewer Pauli terms and a shallower circuit. Let's build Hamiltonians for the full valence space and the autoCAS-EOS space. Compare the integral statistics in `get_summary()`.

notebookCells.heading: Compare Hamiltonians for two active space sizes
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Compare Hamiltonians for two active space sizes

sections.title: Part 3: What qubit mappers are available?
sections.content:
Part 3: What qubit mappers are available?

The QDK provides two mapper implementations, each supporting a different set of encodings: | Mapper | Encodings | |---|---| | `qdk` (native) | Jordan-Wigner, Bravyi-Kitaev | | `qiskit` (plugin) | Jordan-Wigner, Bravyi-Kitaev, **Parity** | **Jordan-Wigner**: maps each spin-orbital to one qubit; the anti-commutation relation is enforced by Z-string parity chains. Intuitive, but Z-strings grow with system size. **Bravyi-Kitaev**: encodes both occupation and parity in a tree structure; reduces the average Z-string length to O(log n). Often preferred for circuits. **Parity**: reorganizes the encoding around the parity of occupied orbitals; allows two qubits to be tapered off for particle-number-conserving Hamiltonians. Now, run the cell to see both mapper settings.

notebookCells.heading: List available qubit mappers and their encoding options
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: List available qubit mappers and their encoding options

sections.title: Part 4: Applying all encoding schemes
sections.content:
Part 4: Applying all encoding schemes

All five encoding variants are applied to the same autoCAS-EOS Hamiltonian. The output table shows qubit count, Pauli term count, and Schatten norm for each. For this small, highly symmetric system (N₂, 4 orbitals, 8 qubits) the Pauli term counts are identical across encodings. For larger, less symmetric molecules the counts diverge — BK typically reduces terms relative to JW for mid-sized systems. The **Schatten norm** (sum of |Pauli coefficients|) is encoding-independent: it is a property of the Hamiltonian, not its representation. It sets the maximum useful evolution time in QPE. In the cell bellow, fill in the missing encoding name, then run the cell.

notebookCells.heading: Apply all encoding schemes to the autoCAS-EOS Hamiltonian
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Apply all encoding schemes to the autoCAS-EOS Hamiltonian

quiz.note: Question hosted in qBook question bank (ID: ch4-q1)

sections.title: Part 5: Verifying energy agreement and solver comparison
sections.content:
Part 5: Verifying energy agreement and solver comparison

All encodings are unitary-equivalent representations of the same operator: their ground-state eigenvalues must agree exactly. This cell runs exact diagonalization on every encoding and checks agreement, then compares the dense and sparse matrix solvers. The **dense solver** builds the full 2ⁿ× 2ⁿ Hilbert space matrix explicitly. This is simple but uses O(4ⁿ) memory. For 8 qubits (2⁸ = 256) it is fast; for 16 qubits (2¹⁶ = 65536) it is slow. The **sparse solver** uses iterative Lanczos/Davidson methods, only storing the non-zero Hamiltonian entries. Much more efficient for larger qubit counts; the recommended default for n > 12 qubits. In the cell below, choose which solver to use and then confirm all encodings agree.

notebookCells.heading: Verify energy agreement across encodings and compare solvers
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Verify energy agreement across encodings and compare solvers

sections.title: Part 6: Schatten norm and QPE circuit cost
sections.content:
Part 6: Schatten norm and QPE circuit cost

The Schatten (1-)norm of the qubit Hamiltonian is the sum of the absolute values of all Pauli coefficients. In first-order Trotterized QPE, the maximum useful evolution time is bounded by π / norm. A larger norm forces shorter evolution steps, which requires more Trotter repetitions to achieve a target precision — directly increasing circuit depth on Magne. This is the quantitative link between active space selection (Chapter 3) and QPE cost (Chapter 6): a larger active space has larger coefficients, a larger norm, and a shorter evolution time per step.

notebookCells.heading: Schatten norm: connecting Hamiltonian to QPE circuit cost
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Schatten norm: connecting Hamiltonian to QPE circuit cost

sections.title: Part 7: Model Hamiltonians
sections.content:
Part 7: Model Hamiltonians

Beyond molecular systems, the QDK provides built-in **model Hamiltonians** that are widely used as testbeds for quantum algorithms and near-term hardware benchmarks: | Model | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | Ising | Spin (direct QubitHamiltonian) | ZZ coupling + transverse field; simplest benchmark | | Heisenberg | Spin (direct QubitHamiltonian) | XX+YY+ZZ coupling; models magnetic systems | | Hubbard | Fermionic → qubit mapping | Hopping + on-site repulsion; minimal metal model | Spin models produce a `QubitHamiltonian` directly — no fermionic mapping step needed. Fermionic models (Hubbard, Hückel, PPP) follow the **same** `hamiltonian_constructor → qubit_mapper` pipeline as molecular systems, making them ideal minimal testbeds for circuit design. Connectivity is specified via `LatticeGraph` — use `LatticeGraph.chain(n)` for an open chain and `LatticeGraph.chain(n, periodic=True)` for a ring. Fill in the encoding name in the cell below.

notebookCells.heading: Model Hamiltonians: Ising, Heisenberg, and Hubbard
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Model Hamiltonians: Ising, Heisenberg, and Hubbard

sections.title: Summary
sections.content:
Summary

In this chapter you: - Built classical Hamiltonians for two active spaces and compared how integral counts scale with n⁴ - Listed and inspected all available qubit mappers and their encoding settings - Applied Jordan-Wigner, Bravyi-Kitaev, and Parity encodings and confirmed they produce the same spectrum - Computed the Schatten norm and connected it to QPE circuit depth — the bridge to Chapter 6 - Built model Hamiltonians (Ising, Heisenberg, Hubbard) using `LatticeGraph` and compared spin vs. fermionic construction paths **Key pattern:** ```python ham_constructor = create("hamiltonian_constructor") active_ham = ham_constructor.run(wfn_eos.get_orbitals()) qubit_mapper = create("qubit_mapper", "qdk", encoding="jordan-wigner") qubit_ham = qubit_mapper.run(active_ham) solver = create("qubit_hamiltonian_solver", "qdk_sparse_matrix_solver") energy, _ = solver.run(qubit_ham) ``` The `active_ham` and `qubit_ham` objects are carried forward into Chapter 5 (state preparation).

sections.title: Chapter 5: State Preparation
sections.content:
Chapter 5: State Preparation

What you'll learn

- The two isometry-based state preparers in the QDK and their settings - Why a single Slater determinant (HF reference) requires only X gates — depth 1 - How circuit depth scales with the number of determinants in a multi-configurational state - Why sparse isometry outperforms the regular (dense) isometry by orders of magnitude - How to export a prepared state circuit to QASM, QIR, Qiskit, and Q# formats

From wavefunction to quantum register

The previous chapters produced a classical wavefunction description: either a single Slater determinant (HF) or a superposition of many determinants (SCI). Before quantum phase estimation can run, this classical description must be encoded into an actual quantum circuit that maps |00...0⟩ to the target state. This is **state preparation** (also called isometry synthesis). The QDK provides two approaches: | Preparer | Strategy | When to use | |---|---|---| | `sparse_isometry_gf2x` | GF2X elimination on the sparse binary amplitude matrix | Few-determinant states (HF, truncated SCI) | | `qiskit_regular_isometry` | Full dense unitary decomposition | Only for toy systems (<4 qubits) | The sparse method is the practical choice for any real chemistry calculation. Circuit depth grows with the number of dominant determinants — not with the size of the full Hilbert space — making state preparation the stage where classical wavefunction truncation pays off directly in hardware cost.

notebookCells.heading: Setup: SCF → valence → MP2 → autoCAS-EOS pipeline
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Setup: SCF → valence → MP2 → autoCAS-EOS pipeline

sections.title: Part 1: What state preparers are available?
sections.content:
Part 1: What state preparers are available?

`available("state_prep")` lists both preparers. `print_settings()` shows the configurable parameters: `transpile`, `basis_gates`, and (for sparse only) `dense_preparation_method`. Run the cell to see them.

notebookCells.heading: List available state preparers and their settings
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: List available state preparers and their settings

sections.title: Part 2: Single Slater determinant -- HF reference
sections.content:
Part 2: Single Slater determinant — HF reference

The simplest state to prepare is the Hartree-Fock reference: a single Slater determinant. Under Jordan-Wigner encoding, this is just a computational basis state with |1⟩ for each occupied spin-orbital and |0⟩ for each empty one. Preparing it from |00...0⟩ requires only **X gates** — no entanglement. Circuit depth is 1, regardless of system size. Fill in the preparer name in the cell below.

notebookCells.heading: Single Slater determinant: HF reference state preparation
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Single Slater determinant: HF reference state preparation

sections.title: Part 3: Scaling with number of determinants
sections.content:
Part 3: Scaling with number of determinants

Real chemistry wavefunctions have many determinants. The SCI result for N₂ has 3136 — requiring a depth-194,000 circuit if prepared exactly. In practice, the dominant determinants carry most of the wavefunction weight: keeping only the top-10 reduces depth to ~314 with minimal state error. The `truncate_wavefunction` utility sorts determinants by |coefficient| and keeps the top-N, renormalized. Run the cell to see how depth scales.

notebookCells.heading: Scaling of state preparation depth with number of determinants
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Scaling of state preparation depth with number of determinants

sections.title: Part 4: Sparse vs. regular isometry
sections.content:
Part 4: Sparse vs. regular isometry

`sparse_isometry_gf2x` uses GF2X (Gaussian elimination over GF(2) augmented with X operations) to find a compact CNOT+X circuit. It exploits the sparsity of the amplitude matrix — only the support of the wavefunction matters. `qiskit_regular_isometry` performs a full dense unitary decomposition. It is general-purpose but its cost is proportional to the full 2ⁿ-dimensional Hilbert space, not the support size. For the same 10-determinant state: depth 314 vs. 337,756. Fill in the preparer name to complete the comparison.

notebookCells.heading: Sparse vs. regular isometry on the same 10-determinant state
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Sparse vs. regular isometry on the same 10-determinant state

sections.title: Part 5: Circuit export formats
sections.content:
Part 5: Circuit export formats

A prepared `Circuit` object can be exported in four formats: - `get_qasm()` — OpenQASM 3.0 string (hardware-agnostic, human-readable) - `get_qiskit_circuit()` — Qiskit `QuantumCircuit` (for noise simulation or transpilation) - `get_qsharp_circuit()` — Q# circuit (for QDK resource estimation) - `get_qir()` — QIR bitcode (compiled, hardware-ready for Azure Quantum / Magne) QIR (Quantum Intermediate Representation) is the native compiled format for Magne — the format you submit when running on hardware.

notebookCells.heading: Circuit export: QASM, Qiskit, Q#, and QIR
notebookCells.warning: No notebook match for h3: Circuit export: QASM, Qiskit, Q#, and QIR

sections.title: Summary
sections.content:
Summary

In this chapter you: - Listed the two state preparers (`sparse_isometry_gf2x`, `qiskit_regular_isometry`) and their settings - Prepared the HF reference state and confirmed it produces a depth-1 X-gate circuit - Built a `truncate_wavefunction` utility and measured how circuit depth grows with determinant count - Compared sparse vs. regular isometry on a 10-determinant state (depth 314 vs. 337,756) - Exported the prepared circuit to QASM, Qiskit, Q#, and QIR formats **Key pattern:** ```python from qdk_chemistry._core.data import SciWavefunctionContainer from qdk_chemistry.data import Wavefunction # Truncate to top-N determinants wfn_10 = truncate_wavefunction(wfn_sci, 10) # Prepare circuit sp = create("state_prep", "sparse_isometry_gf2x") circ = sp.run(wfn_10) # Inspect and export qc = circ.get_qiskit_circuit() print(qc.count_ops()) qasm = circ.get_qasm() ``` The `wfn_10` and `circ_10` are carried forward into Chapter 6 (QPE).
